CLARITY.justification: The paper contains the required sections (related work, method, experiments) but is poorly organized and contains large amounts of duplicated, garbled text (e.g. repeated TempEval/TWn paragraphs and duplicated citation strings), which breaks the logical flow. The main contribution is stated in places but not cleanly or early (scattered mentions of a semi‑supervised min‑cut approach). Notation is minimal and no consistent definitions are presented; figures/tables are referenced (Table 2) but not shown here and examples/annotations are described unevenly. Writing has numerous grammatical issues, awkward phrasing, and editorial errors (missing/misplaced punctuation, repeated sentences, and unexplained footnote markers) that hinder readability.

APPROPRIATENESS.justification: This work addresses a clearly NLP-centered problem (time/temporality of WordNet synsets) and proposes a semi-supervised graph-based (min-cut) method that leverages WordNet relations and glosses — topics that are squarely within top NLP venues. The paper includes appropriate data construction (crowdsourced gold standard), intrinsic and extrinsic evaluations (mentions TempEval-3 comparisons and use of Lesk WSD), and compares to prior TempoWordNet approaches, so the methods and evaluation meet venue expectations. Minor concerns are that the core algorithmic contribution is an application of established graph-based semi-supervised techniques rather than a novel ML method, so it is a stronger fit for top NLP conferences than for a top-tier general ML venue.

IMPACT.justification: The paper tackles a relevant problem—assigning temporal orientation to WordNet synsets—which has clear applications in IR, QA and summarization (paper cites many temporal-NLP/IR tasks). Its main contribution (a semi‑supervised graph-based mincut method that exploits WordNet relations, glosses and other attributes) and a crowdsourced gold standard address shortcomings of prior TempoWordNet approaches and reportedly outperform non‑graph semi‑supervised methods. This is likely to influence researchers working on lexical temporal resources and inspire further graph‑based sense‑level temporality work, but it is an incremental methodological advance with somewhat niche uptake and clear limits (ambiguity of temporality and fuzzy class boundaries), so its impact is moderate rather than transformative.

REPLICABILITY.justification: Key dataset sources are named (WordNet; 632 temporal + 632 atemporal synsets from Dias et al., and a 398-synset CrowdFlower annotation) so data provenance is partly clear, but annotation guidelines are omitted (“Details of annotation guideline is out of scope of this paper”) and the CrowdFlower setup lacks exact worker/filtering parameters. The core algorithm is only described at a high level (“semi-supervised minimum cuts”, “max-flow min-cut theorem”) with no specifics on graph construction, edge weights, feature extraction from glosses, hyperparameters, solver/implementation details or stopping criteria, so model/configuration choices cannot be reproduced. Evaluation reporting gives agreement statistics (e.g. 84.83% and 72.36%) but no precise evaluation metrics/procedures, thresholds, or code, and no links to datasets or code are provided, making faithful reimplementation difficult.

ORIGINALITY.justification: The paper applies a semi-supervised graph-based (max-flow/min-cut) paradigm to the temporality labeling of WordNet synsets, which the authors claim is the first such graph-based approach for word-sense temporality (paper repeatedly states “first work... via graph-based classification”). This is clearly differentiated from prior TempoWordNet work (Dias et al., 2014) and subsequent propagation variants (Hasanuzzaman et al.) that mainly relied on gloss statistics and SVM-based probabilities; here the method explicitly exploits WordNet relations, glosses and other attributes within a graph formulation. The technique is a non-trivial application/combination (optimization-based min-cut on a lexical graph plus a new crowdsourced gold standard) rather than a mere re-run of known baselines, so it represents notable novelty though not a fundamentally new algorithmic invention.

SUBSTANCE.justification: The paper provides nontrivial experimental effort (uses 632 labeled temporal and 632 atemporal synsets from Dias et al. (2014), builds a 398‑synset crowd‑annotated gold standard with agreement statistics 84.8% / 72.4%) and claims intrinsic and extrinsic evaluations where their method outperforms prior non‑graph approaches. Related work is surveyed broadly (TempEval, TempoWordNet, TWnP/TWnH, graph and semi‑supervised methods are discussed) and the authors acknowledge task difficulties and ambiguity (example: “dinosaur”, and issues distinguishing past/present/future). However, the excerpt shows little detail on quantitative results, baseline comparisons, algorithmic and hyperparameter settings, or systematic ablations/sensitivity analyses (aside from a brief note that Lesk was used to test WSD contribution), so reproducibility and depth of analysis are incomplete.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: The paper compares to the relevant prior work on temporality (Dias et al., 2014 / TempoWordNet, Hasanuzzaman et al. 2014a/b, Filannino & Nenadic 2014) and states it uses the same labeled data from Dias et al. for training (Section 4.1) and reports intrinsic/extrinsic evaluation claiming to outperform prior semi‑supervised non‑graph approaches. It also discusses methodological differences (use of WordNet relations vs. glosses) and critiques prior evaluation/calibration choices. However, the excerpt does not show detailed quantitative re‑evaluations of competing methods on the new gold standard or thorough fairness analysis (e.g., whether baselines were re-run or probability calibration applied), so comparisons appear good but with minor omissions in reported experimental parity.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: The paper clearly states its main claim (a semi‑supervised min‑cut / graph based method to time‑tag WordNet synsets that reportedly outperforms prior semi‑supervised, non‑graph approaches), and describes datasets and a crowdsourced gold standard (632 labeled temporal/atemporal synsets from Dias et al., plus a 398‑synset crowd sample with controls). However, the provided excerpt contains no concrete experimental results, metrics, comparison tables, or statistical significance tests to substantiate the outperforming claim, and no mathematical derivations of the proposed optimization beyond a citation of max‑flow/min‑cut theory. While the annotation methodology and use of prior labeled data are reasonable, missing details about baselines, calibration (criticized earlier but not addressed), and quantitative results leave important technical gaps, so the work is plausible but incompletely supported by the evidence shown.
